glossary.def: The raw, one-dimensional signal a microphone produces: a sequence of amplitude samples taken at a fixed sample rate (16 kHz for speech, 44.1/48 kHz for music). It is a faithful but punishingly long representation — one second of 16 kHz mono audio is 16,000 numbers — and locally almost meaningless, which is why audio models first transform it into something shorter and more structured.

A 2-D image of an audio signal with time on one axis and frequency on the other, produced by taking the magnitude of a Short-Time Fourier Transform of overlapping windowed frames. Brightness encodes energy at each time-frequency point. Phase is usually discarded, so a vocoder must re-synthesize it when reconstructing audio.

Sliding a window of N samples across a waveform (hopping by H samples each step) and taking the Fourier transform of each frame. The window length N sets the frequency resolution (df = fs/N) and the time resolution (dt = N/fs); because their product is fixed at 1, a long window resolves frequency well but blurs time, and a short window does the reverse — the audio form of the Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty limit.

A perceptual frequency scale, m(f) = 2595 log10(1 + f/700), that approximates how human pitch perception compresses high frequencies. Filterbank bins spaced uniformly in mel widen geometrically in Hz, matching the ear's declining resolution. Warping a spectrogram onto ~80 mel bands and taking the log gives the log-mel spectrogram, the input consumed by almost every modern speech model.

A convolutional autoencoder (SoundStream, EnCodec) that compresses a waveform to a low-rate latent and quantizes it with residual vector quantization: a stack of Q codebooks, each quantizing the residual the previous one left behind. Stacking Q codebooks of K entries yields K^Q effective combinations at only Q*log2(K) bits, turning continuous audio into a grid of discrete tokens a language model can predict.

OpenAI's encoder-decoder transformer for speech recognition (2022), trained on 680,000 hours of weakly labelled multilingual web audio. The encoder ingests an 80-bin log-mel spectrogram and the decoder autoregressively predicts text, with special prompt tokens selecting the task (transcribe, translate, timestamps). Its robustness comes from scale rather than architecture; its failure mode is hallucinating fluent text on silent or unintelligible input.

The second stage of neural text-to-speech: a model that converts a mel spectrogram into a waveform, re-inventing the phase the spectrogram discarded. WaveNet (2016) did this autoregressively with dilated causal convolutions — high fidelity but one sample at a time — and was largely replaced by GAN vocoders such as HiFi-GAN that generate an entire waveform in a single non-autoregressive forward pass.

A transformer trained to predict the next audio token over a neural-codec vocabulary, exactly as a text LLM predicts the next word. AudioLM showed it can continue speech or music coherently — preserving speaker, prosody, and room acoustics — with no transcript. Models often split tokens into coarse semantic and fine acoustic streams, and text-conditioned variants (MusicGen, VALL-E) prepend text or a reference clip; frontier omni models emit audio tokens directly with no separate ASR or TTS stage.

refs.cite: Radford, A., Kim, J. W., Xu, T., Brockman, G., McLeavey, C. & Sutskever, I. (2022). Robust Speech Recognition via Large-Scale Weak Supervision. OpenAI.
van den Oord, A. et al. (2016). WaveNet: A Generative Model for Raw Audio. DeepMind.
Defossez, A., Copet, J., Synnaeve, G. & Adi, Y. (2022). High Fidelity Neural Audio Compression (EnCodec). Meta.
Shen, J. et al. (2018). Natural TTS Synthesis by Conditioning WaveNet on Mel Spectrogram Predictions (Tacotron 2). Google.
Ren, Y. et al. (2021). FastSpeech 2: Fast and High-Quality End-to-End Text to Speech. ICLR 2021.
Baevski, A., Zhou, H., Mohamed, A. & Auli, M. (2020). wav2vec 2.0: A Framework for Self-Supervised Learning of Speech Representations. Meta.
Borsos, Z. et al. (2022). AudioLM: A Language Modeling Approach to Audio Generation. Google.
